items.title: fix(kvbm): query NCCL version at runtime instead of hardcoding
items.body:
## Summary
Replace hardcoded `version: 22800` in `ncclConfig_t` with a runtime `ncclGetVersion()` call.

## Problem
`nccl_bootstrap.rs` hardcodes the NCCL config version to `22800` (NCCL 2.28.0). The current `cuda-dl-base` containers ship **NCCL 2.29.2** (version `22902`). `ncclCommInitRankConfig` rejects the config struct with `ncclInvalidUsage` because the version field doesn't match the runtime library.

This completely breaks `DYN_KVBM_NCCL_MLA_MODE=true` — rank 0 fails at comm init, ranks 1-3 hang in MPI bcast, and the ZMQ leader/worker handshake times out.

Verified on Lyris GB200 (aarch64):
```bash
srun --container-image=<trtllm-runtime-kvbm-nccl.sqsh> bash -c \
  "python3 -c \"import ctypes; nccl=ctypes.CDLL('libnccl.so.2'); v=ctypes.c_int(); nccl.ncclGetVersion(ctypes.byref(v)); print(v.value)\""
# Output: 22902  (NCCL 2.29.2)
```

## Fix
Query `ncclGetVersion()` at runtime and use the result for the config struct. One line change, forward-compatible with any NCCL version.

## Test plan
- [ ] CI build passes (Rust compilation with nccl feature)
- [ ] KVBM NCCL MLA mode initializes successfully on TRT-LLM TP4 (needs GPU CI)

items.title: feat: Python rewrite of relational2rdf (SIARD → RDF Turtle)
items.body:
Full Python reimplementation of the .NET `Relational2Rdf` tool under `python/`. All existing functionality is preserved: SIARD v1/v2/v2.1 parsing, Ontology converter, AI-assisted converter, and multi-threaded orchestration.

## Core modules

- **`siard/`** — Pure-Python XML parsers for SIARD v1 (`v1.py`) and v2/v2.1 (`v2.py`) with auto-detected version, `SiardDataSource` wiring UDT back-references, and a streaming `iterparse`-based table reader
- **`models.py`** — `Protocol`-based abstractions (`IRow`, `ITable`, `ISchema`, `IRelationalDataSource`, …) replacing the C# interface hierarchy
- **`rdf/writer.py`** — `rdflib`-backed Turtle writer with typed literal generation
- **`converter/ontology/`** — Fixed SIARD ontology converter (Row → Cell → Column graph with Reference objects for foreign keys)
- **`converter/ai/`** — LLM inference backends (`OpenAIInference`, `OllamaInference`) + prompt builders (`AiMagic`) + async table converter with M:M junction-table detection
- **`converter/manager.py`** — `ThreadPoolExecutor` for Ontology mode; `asyncio.gather` with semaphore for AI mode
- **`cli.py`** — `typer` CLI mirroring all original flags

## LM Studio support

LM Studio works out of the box via the `openai` service type — no extra code path needed:

```bash
relational2rdf siard archive.siard \
  --converter ai \
  --ai-service openai \
  --ai-endpoint http://localhost:1234/ \
  --ai-model "your-loaded-model" \
  --ai-key lm-studio \
  --table-config table-config.json
```

## Dependencies

`typer`, `rdflib`, `openai`, `httpx`, `lxml` — all pure-Python, no native extensions required.

items.title: Phase 4 FFI: Add POINTER(T), str/bytes coercion, c_char_p returns, and string_at
items.body:
## Summary

Extends Phase 4 ctypes FFI support with composite pointer types, string/bytes parameter coercion, c_char_p return value handling, and the `ctypes.string_at()` function. Adds comprehensive type checking for function pointer calls and enables `Any` as a first-class opaque pointer type in the type system.

## Key Changes

### Type System & Analyzer (`pyx/analyzer.py`, `pyx/type_system.py`)
- **POINTER(T) support**: `_resolve_ctypes_type()` now recognizes `ctypes.POINTER(T)` calls and maps them to `"c_void_p"` (opaque pointer)
- **c_char_p → bytes mapping**: `ctypes_to_pyx_type()` now returns `"bytes"` for `c_char_p` (C strings are converted to length-bearing bytes via strlen)
- **Parameter type checking**: New `_check_ctypes_arg_compat()` validates PyX argument types against ctypes parameter expectations:
  - `int` ↔ integer ctypes types
  - `float` ↔ float ctypes types  
  - `str`/`bytes` ↔ `c_char_p`
  - `Any` accepted for all pointer types
- **string_at() support**: New `_infer_string_at_call()` type-checks `ctypes.string_at(ptr, size)` → `bytes`
- **Any type validation**: `is_supported_type("Any")` now returns True (opaque pointer support)

### Compiler (`pyx/compiler.py`)
- **POINTER(T) lowering**: `_resolve_ctypes_type_name()` maps `POINTER(T)` to `"c_void_p"` for LLVM IR generation
- **str/bytes → c_char_p coercion**: `_coerce_to_ctype()` extracts data pointers via `extractvalue` from `%pyx.str` and `%pyx.bytes` structs
- **c_char_p → bytes conversion**: `_coerce_from_ctype()` uses `strlen()` to determine length, then malloc+memcpy to create a `%pyx.bytes` struct
- **string_at() compilation**: New `_compile_string_at()` method implements `ctypes.string_at(ptr, size)` by allocating a buffer, copying data via memcpy, and returning a bytes struct
- **Any type support**: `llvm_type()` now maps `"Any"` to `"ptr"` (opaque pointer in LLVM IR)
- **strlen declaration**: Added `uses_strlen` flag and preamble emission for strlen when needed

### Tests
- **15 new analyzer tests** covering POINTER(T), str/bytes coercion, c_char_p returns, and string_at
- **15 new compiler tests** verifying LLVM IR generation for pointer handling, struct extraction, strlen usage, and malloc/memcpy patterns
- All 108 tests pass (93 existing + 15 new Phase 4 tests)

## Implementation Details

- **POINTER(T) → ptr**: All composite pointer types unify to LLVM `ptr` (no per-type tracking yet)
- **c_char_p return path**: Uses `strlen()` to discover C string length, then heap-allocates and copies to preserve ownership semantics
- **str/bytes parameters**: Data pointers extracted via `extractvalue` struct field access; no automatic null-termination added
- **string_at semantics**: Caller provides explicit size; function performs malloc+memcpy to create independent bytes value
- **Type checking**: Function pointer calls now validate argument compatibility before code generation

## Documentation Updates

Updated ROADMAP.md to reflect Phase 4 extension completion with 40 total Phase 4 tests, clarified memory ownership semantics for c_char_p conversions, and documented remaining work (null-termination helpers, POINTER(T) type preservation, byref/addressof, cast, struct support).

items.title: ⚡ Optimize ctypes array creation in Packet.__to_buffers
items.body:
💡 **What:** Caches the `ctypes` buffer array in `Packet` instances during `__to_buffers` execution using the buffer's length and `id()`.
🎯 **Why:** To eliminate the Python-level overhead of repeatedly calling `(ctypes.c_char * len).from_buffer(buff)` when a packet's payload and underlying buffer haven't changed.
📊 **Measured Improvement:** In a microbenchmark of 1,000,000 calls to `__to_buffers` with a 1500-byte packet, the cached version took ~0.43 seconds, compared to the unoptimized baseline of ~1.51 seconds, resulting in a ~3.5x speedup.

- **Docker Compose** — all commands run inside Docker; `docker compose run --rm test` / `docker compose run --rm app`

### Motivation
- Provide a Windows `ctypes`-based wrapper around `airspy.dll` so the project can directly open/configure Airspy devices and collect IQ samples into NumPy. 
- Replace the previous direct dependency in `find_capsule.py` with a SoapySDR/Pothos-based reader for better portability, improved stream handling, and clearer gain mapping. 
- Improve robustness and resource cleanup when streaming IQ data and handling missing dependencies.

### Description
- Add `airspy_wrapper.py` implementing an `AirSpy` class that loads `airspy.dll` via `ctypes`, exposes `open`, `close`, `set_frequency`, `set_sample_rate`, `set_lna_gain`, `set_vga_gain`, `start`, and `stop`, converts interleaved int16 IQ to `complex64` NumPy arrays, and includes a small demo in `__main__`.
- Rewrite `find_capsule.py` to use `SoapySDR` (PothosSDR) by introducing `PothosAirspyReader` with `configure`, `capture`, and `close` methods, plus `measure_power` and `gain_to_lna_vga` helpers; add constants `SAMPLE_RATE` and `STREAM_TIMEOUT_US` and chunked streaming logic with timeout handling.
- Update CLI messaging, add better error messages and tips when SoapySDR initialization fails, and ensure streams are deactivated/closed in `finally` blocks.
- Validate and clamp user gain into LNA/VGA components and switch default sample rate to 2_500_000 for the PothosSDR path.

### Testing
- Ran project linting (`flake8`) against the modified files and fixed style issues; linter passed for these changes.
- Executed automated CI import and help invocation check by running `python find_capsule.py --help` in the CI environment; the invocation completed successfully.
- Performed a CI smoke runtime test in a SoapySDR-enabled environment that configured the device, captured samples with `PothosAirspyReader.capture`, and computed power values; the capture and power computation succeeded.

## @vultisig/mpc-native@0.1.3

### Patch Changes

-   [#267](https://github.com/vultisig/vultisig-sdk/pull/267) [`91aa66a`](https://github.com/vultisig/vultisig-sdk/commit/91aa66a0c23576546895d0946b486ae37cf1b23d) Thanks [@gomesalexandre](https://github.com/gomesalexandre)! - fix(mpc-native): make Android build green and runtime-functional

    The Android side of `@vultisig/mpc-native` was non-functional end-to-end:
    the Kotlin wrapper imported `godkls.Godkls` / `goschnorr.Goschnorr` (a
    gomobile-style API that doesn't exist in the shipped AARs), the
    `godklsJNI`/`goschnorrJNI` SWIG classes had no static `loadLibrary` initializer
    so the underlying `.so` files were never resolved, and AGP 9+ refuses to
    package the prebuilt AARs as `implementation` deps inside an AAR module
    (`bundleDebugAar` fails with "Direct local .aar file dependencies are not
    supported"). The result was that any consumer trying to import this package on
    Android hit a Gradle config failure during build, and even a hand-patched
    build crashed at first JNI call with `UnsatisfiedLinkError` /
    `LIB_SERIALIZATION_ERROR`. This change makes the Android side actually work:

    -   **Kotlin wrapper rewritten against the real SWIG bindings.** All
        `Godkls.*`/`Goschnorr.*` calls replaced with the actual
        `com.silencelaboratories.godkls.godkls.dkls_*` /
        `com.silencelaboratories.goschnorr.goschnorr.schnorr_*` static methods.
        Helper closures wrap the `tss_buffer` / `go_slice` / `Handle` lifecycle
        with `try/finally` cleanup; a per-module `dklsHandles` /`schnorrHandles`
        Long→Handle map preserves the existing JS-side handle ID surface. Optional
        inputs (`keyId`, `messageHash`, `rootChainCode`) pass `null` (matching iOS
        Swift) instead of an empty slice, and QC sessions for the no-keyshare /
        old-party path use `Handle().apply { set_0(-1) }` (mirroring iOS
        `Handle(_0: Int32(keyshareHandle ?? -1))`) so the C side branches correctly.
    -   **Static `System.loadLibrary` initializer.** A `companion object { init { … } }`
        loads `godkls`, `goschnorr`, `godklsswig`, `goschnorrswig` in dependency
        order so the SWIG `.so` files find their underlying Go libs already in the
        linker namespace. Wrapped in `try/catch` so a failed load surfaces a clean
        error to JS at first call instead of crashing the host app on launch.
    -   **Switched local AARs to `compileOnly`.** Direct `implementation files(libs/*.aar)`
        is forbidden by AGP 9+ inside an AAR module. Using `compileOnly` lets the
        Kotlin wrapper still see the SWIG classes for compilation; runtime
        packaging is handled at the consuming **app** module via the new Expo
        config plugin shipped at `app.plugin.js` (so consumers just add
        `@vultisig/mpc-native` to their `app.json` `plugins` array and the AARs
        end up on the final APK's runtime classpath).
    -   **Replaced the prebuilt Android AARs.** The AARs in this package were
        shipping a stripped/old `dkls-android` build whose `libgoschnorrswig.so`
        carried an absolute-path `DT_NEEDED` baked from the original build host
        (so `dlopen` of the SWIG lib failed at runtime on real devices), and
        whose `libgoschnorr.so` rejected the FROST keyshare format used by
        Vultisig vaults with `LIB_SERIALIZATION_ERROR` (Solana / Cardano / other
        EdDSA chains couldn't sign). Replaced with the production-grade builds
        shipped by the `vultisig/vultisig-android` app at `app/libs/`, which have
        no absolute-path issue and accept the keyshare format end-to-end.

items.title: feat: harden swig session settlement lifecycle + add swig session demo
items.body:
## Summary
- Hardened session settlement semantics across the SDK by carrying `closeTx` through close authorization and server verification, and by adding optional `settleOnLimitHit` behavior when channel limits are reached.
- Created a Swig demo which uses USDC base units end-to-end (pricing, deposits, settlement transfers, and verifier checks) and aligned all Swig references to the real Swig program id.
- Delegated-role lifecycle in the demo creates a fresh delegated Swig role per session, removing that role on successful close settlement, and reinitializing session state so reopened channels do not reuse stale role limits.

## Implementation Details
- Added `closeTx` support in session method schema/types/client/server and added `verifyClose(...)` handling on the server path.
- Added `settleOnLimitHit?: boolean` to the session client so over-limit requests can auto-close/settle instead of throwing when `autoTopup` is disabled.
- Swig demo server enforces USDC SPL asset semantics and validates open/topup/close transactions against expected token transfer behavior.
- Swig demo client now:
  - builds open/topup/close USDC token-transfer flows,
  - uses delegated-role-scoped signing for settlement,
  - recycles delegated roles by removing them on successful close,
  - detects and recreates legacy Swigs with session-based manager role config.
- Updated docs and demo UX text to reflect Swig session + USDC behavior, plus delegated role visibility in the demo UI.
## Validation
- `npm run typecheck`
- `npx tsc --noEmit` (demo app)
- `npm run build` (demo app)
- manually tested demo end 2 end

items.title: Fix background mode cursor disruption, broken hotkeys; add Ctrl+Alt+I toggle, UI refresh
items.body:
Background mode was physically moving the user's cursor via pynput's sequential move/click/restore, visibly disrupting mouse usage. Global hotkeys via `GlobalHotKeys` silently failed to register. UI needed polish and author attribution (王五一).

## Changes

- **Background clicking (Windows)**: Replace pynput `Controller` with batched Win32 `SendInput` via ctypes — 4 mouse events (move, down, up, restore) dispatched in a single syscall, reducing cursor displacement to microseconds. Non-Windows falls back to pynput.

```python
# Atomic batch: move → click → restore in one SendInput call
events = (INPUT * 4)()
events[0].mi.dwFlags = MOUSEEVENTF_MOVE | MOUSEEVENTF_ABSOLUTE  # move to target
events[1].mi.dwFlags = MOUSEEVENTF_LEFTDOWN
events[2].mi.dwFlags = MOUSEEVENTF_LEFTUP
events[3].mi.dwFlags = MOUSEEVENTF_MOVE | MOUSEEVENTF_ABSOLUTE  # restore cursor
ctypes.windll.user32.SendInput(4, ctypes.byref(events[0]), ctypes.sizeof(INPUT))
```

- **Hotkey detection**: Replace `GlobalHotKeys` with `pynput.keyboard.Listener` + manual pressed-key tracking via `_pressed_keys` set. Normalizes modifier keys, falls back to `key.vk` for character keys when `key.char` is `None` (e.g. when Alt is held). Cooldown flag prevents repeat-firing until a key is released.

- **Ctrl+Alt+I hotkey**: New global shortcut toggles background mode on/off, wired through the same keyboard listener.

- **UI**: Themed header with title + author credit (王五一), animated pulsing status dot (sine-wave green channel), `clam` ttk theme, consistent color constants.

items.title: bench: ctypes-load librac_dsp_core.so — real RAC-DSP per-op timing
items.body:
Before: the "RAC-DSP C ref" row in bench_three_paths.py shelled out to sim/rac_systolic_ref, so the 0.71 ms/call measurement was dominated by fork/exec/mmap overhead. Totally fine as a correctness oracle (just counts wall-clock), useless as a perf data point.

After: a new ctypes path loads sim/librac_dsp_core.so directly and calls rac_dsp_eval / rac_dsp_project_batch / rac_dsp_project_sum in process. Real per-rotation timing with ~100 ns of Python↔C ffi overhead instead of ~1 ms of subprocess spawn.

New batch helpers in rac_dsp_core.{h,c}
  rac_dsp_project_batch(n, xs, zs, xout) — N project evaluations in a
    tight C loop, no ctypes boundary crossing per call. Used for the
    "CORDIC floor" measurement.
  rac_dsp_project_sum(n, xs, zs) -> sum — one column of the systolic
    array in C. Python outer loop calls this N times for an N×N GEMM.

New Makefile target
  make lib          → builds librac_dsp_core.so (-fPIC -shared)

Bench additions
  load_rac_dsp_lib()              → ctypes-load, declare prototypes,
                                    call rac_load_all_roms on the
                                    standard cordic_*.mem files
  time_glu_project_single()       → ns/rotation with one ctypes call
                                    per rotation (boundary-dominated)
  time_glu_project_batch(n=4096)  → ns/rotation inside the C loop
                                    (CORDIC floor)
  time_glu_empirical_sgemm_inproc → ms/call + GFLOPS for N×N×N matmul
                                    using rac_dsp_project_sum per col

Header now prints both numbers up-front so the user sees where the Python-to-C transition tax sits:
  GLU:  8-engine bank @ 200 MHz = 0.62 ns/op
        single rac_dsp_eval  =  1022.9 ns/rotation (ctypes round-trip)
        batch  rac_dsp_eval  =    56.5 ns/rotation (tight C, CORDIC floor)

The SGEMM row now emits:
  RAC-DSP C ref (16³):        0.02 ms/call        0.3 GFLOPS
                              (bit-exact rac_dsp.v mirror via librac_dsp_core.so)

Before commit in sandbox: 0.71 ms/call, 0.0 GFLOPS (subprocess noise)
After  commit in sandbox: 0.02 ms/call, 0.3 GFLOPS (meaningful software floor)

Order-of-magnitude faster, orders-of-magnitude more honest.

Context for the 56 ns/rotation number:
  Each rac_dsp_eval runs 10 coarse + 9 residual = 19 shift-add
  iterations on 64-bit ints. At ~3 ns per iter on a 5950X (L1
  hot, no branching), minimum floor is ~60 ns. 56 ns measured
  means the C code is near that wall.
  RTL silicon target: 1 rotation/cycle @ 200 MHz = 5 ns/rotation
  per engine, with throughput scaling by # engines. Software vs
  hardware gap ~11× per engine — which is the whole point.

items.title: docs(sdk): FFI performance findings — boundary-crossing overhead across SDKs
items.body:
## What

Adds `sdk/FFI_PERFORMANCE.md` documenting measured FFI boundary-crossing overhead across Rust, Python, and JavaScript SDKs.

## Key Findings

The pure cost of crossing from a host language into Rust and back:

| | req_transformer | res_transformer | Total per call |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **JavaScript** (koffi) | +0.32ms | +0.69ms | **+1.01ms** |
| **Python** (ctypes) | +0.31ms | +1.47ms | **+1.78ms** |

- FFI overhead is **0.2–0.3%** of total round-trip time
- Request side is identical across languages (~0.3ms) — payloads are small
- Response side diverges — Python ctypes marshals large byte buffers ~2× slower than koffi
- Measured against live Stripe sandbox with production-shaped payloads
- Timers placed inside ConnectorClient, not the test harness

items.body:
## tl;dr

the android side of \`@vultisig/mpc-native\" was non-functional end-to-end. this pr makes it work — green build on agp 9+, clean \`System.loadLibrary\" order, and full mpc keysign round-trip validated against production vultisig-verifier on mainnet.

picked this up after @NeOMakinG's excellent spike (https://github.com/vultisig/vultisig-android/pull/3584) — thanks for the breadcrumbs ser 🙏

**consumer stack:** once this merges + gets published to npm, the consuming app (\`vultiagent-poc\`) will flip its local \`file:\" pin back to semver. the full stack on the consumer side is:
- vultisig/vultiagent-app#152 — trustwallet maven repo plugin + chore cleanup
- vultisig/vultiagent-app#153 — adopt \`@vultisig/mpc-native\" via local \`file:\" link (← **this pr** unblocks flipping it back to semver)
- vultisig/vultiagent-app#154 — drop \`react-native-fast-pbkdf2\" + add noble-hashes shim
- vultisig/vultiagent-app#151 — decoupled: in-chat vault share import remount fix

## what was broken

four independent failures, any one of which was fatal:

1. **kotlin wrapper used a fictional api** — imports \`godkls.Godkls\" / \`goschnorr.Goschnorr\" (gomobile-style) but the shipped aars expose the swig bindings at \`com.silencelaboratories.godkls.godkls.dkls_*\`. the package didn't even compile.
2. **no \`System.loadLibrary\" initializer** — \`godklsJNI\`/\`goschnorrJNI\" swig classes have no static init, so \`libgodklsswig.so\" / \`libgoschnorrswig.so\" were never loaded. first jni call → \`UnsatisfiedLinkError\`.
3. **agp 9+ refuses direct \`.aar\" deps inside aar modules** — \`bundleDebugAar\" dies with \"direct local .aar file dependencies are not supported\". even when you hand-patch past the kotlin errors, gradle config fails.
4. **the prebuilt aars were cursed** — \`libgoschnorrswig.so\" had an absolute-path \`DT_NEEDED\" baked in from the original build host (so \`dlopen\" of the swig lib failed at runtime on real devices), and \`libgoschnorr.so\" rejected the frost keyshare format vultisig vaults use with \`LIB_SERIALIZATION_ERROR\" (solana / cardano / other eddsa chains couldn't sign).

## what this pr does

### kotlin wrapper rewritten against the real swig bindings

- all \`Godkls.*\" / \`Goschnorr.*\" calls replaced with the actual \`com.silencelaboratories.godkls.godkls.dkls_*\" / \`com.silencelaboratories.goschnorr.goschnorr.schnorr_*\" static methods
- helper closures wrap the \`tss_buffer\" / \`go_slice\" / \`Handle\" lifecycle with \`try/finally\" cleanup
- per-module \`dklsHandles\" / \`schnorrHandles\" Long→Handle map preserves the existing js-side handle id surface
- optional inputs (\`keyId\`, \`messageHash\`, \`rootChainCode\`) pass \`null\" (matching ios swift) instead of an empty slice
- qc sessions for the no-keyshare / old-party path use \`Handle().apply { set_0(-1) }\" (mirroring ios \`Handle(_0: Int32(keyshareHandle ?? -1))\`) so the c side branches correctly

### static \`System.loadLibrary\" initializer

\`\`\`kotlin
companion object {
    init {
        try {
            System.loadLibrary(\"godkls\")
            System.loadLibrary(\"goschnorr\")
            System.loadLibrary(\"godklsswig\")
            System.loadLibrary(\"goschnorrswig\")
        } catch (e: Throwable) {
            android.util.Log.e(\"ExpoMpcNative\", \"Failed to load native libs\", e)
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

order matters — swig libs link against the underlying go libs, so those need to be in the linker namespace first. wrapped in try/catch so a failed load surfaces a clean error to js at first call instead of crashing the host app on launch.

### switched local aars to \`compileOnly\" + new expo config plugin

direct \`implementation files(libs/*.aar)\" is forbidden by agp 9+ inside an aar module. so:

- **package side** (\`android/build.gradle\`) — declares the aars as \`compileOnly\" so kotlin sees the swig classes for compilation, but they're not packaged into the resulting aar
- **consumer side** — new \`app.plugin.js\" config plugin injects \`implementation files(...)\` lines into the consuming app's \`android/app/build.gradle\" at prebuild time. path is resolved from \`__dirname\` so it works regardless of how npm/pnpm/yarn placed the package on disk (file: link, hoisted, workspace, etc). idempotent via marker comment + crlf-safe regex.

consumers just add \`@vultisig/mpc-native\" to their \`app.json\" plugins array:

\`\`\`json
{
  \"plugins\": [
    \"@vultisig/mpc-native\"
  ]
}
\`\`\`

### replaced the prebuilt android aars

swapped the stripped/old \`dkls-android\" builds for the production-grade builds shipped by the \`vultisig/vultisig-android\" app at \`app/libs/\" — no absolute-path issue, accepts the keyshare format end-to-end. \`libgodkls.so\" goes from 1.1mb to 9.6mb (the old one was missing significant chunks of the dkls protocol).

## testing

validated end-to-end on a google pixel 6 emulator against production vultisig-verifier, with a real vault imported from the vultisig mobile app (consumed via a local \`file:\" link in \`vultisig/vultiagent-app#153\`).

| chain | txid |
|---|---|
| **ETH** (mainnet) | [\`0x80d52a0862743845b39bae94dd04bd8da1d26b8ea53eb6a10097b875cf31c7ae\`](https://etherscan.io/tx/0x80d52a0862743845b39bae94dd04bd8da1d26b8ea53eb6a10097b875cf31c7ae) |
| **RUNE** (thorchain) | [\`92837488E0D35AD5D6ADF6803F407726BE69DB6FB4E2CC135DA7E7C8B9CCE1FA\`](https://viewblock.io/thorchain/tx/92837488E0D35AD5D6ADF6803F407726BE69DB6FB4E2CC135DA7E7C8B9CCE1FA) |
| **SOL** (mainnet) | [\`5cJ7nvdmA9XKKqAfgdBWU6fBSAFoj5cNaJh3vtx5FuZZuvjmv7fcPsCoaQrGjLBYPfZLExrn7HX7DvaxKLgrH7C4\`](https://explorer.solana.com/tx/5cJ7nvdmA9XKKqAfgdBWU6fBSAFoj5cNaJh3vtx5FuZZuvjmv7fcPsCoaQrGjLBYPfZLExrn7HX7DvaxKLgrH7C4) |

eth covers ecdsa keysign via the dkls protocol. rune covers the ecdsa-but-different-derivation-path case. sol covers the eddsa path via frost/schnorr — the one that was hitting \`LIB_SERIALIZATION_ERROR\" with the old aars.

ios side untouched and still green — reproduced self-sends on iphone 16e sim as well:

| chain | txid |
|---|---|
| **ETH** (mainnet) | [\`0x878f26625177a0c981e20c06b420b85b282d3ff13790ce2ec1e53d5018675991\`](https://etherscan.io/tx/0x878f26625177a0c981e20c06b420b85b282d3ff13790ce2ec1e53d5018675991) |
| **RUNE** (thorchain) | [\`F79D4404200ECA3F9C7DE876ECBBC42DFDA7EB9CB52545DA2174FDBEA9A27636\`](https://viewblock.io/thorchain/tx/F79D4404200ECA3F9C7DE876ECBBC42DFDA7EB9CB52545DA2174FDBEA9A27636) |
| **SOL** (mainnet) | [\`2GKFGzXB6ucn2NUdq8ReDRhzqp4ADaZpNkzbx2Cqs2817M8yqvC5Y3AMFzZgGyYhm1HpS6PgwngVnp2u31ZWhqvz\`](https://explorer.solana.com/tx/2GKFGzXB6ucn2NUdq8ReDRhzqp4ADaZpNkzbx2Cqs2817M8yqvC5Y3AMFzZgGyYhm1HpS6PgwngVnp2u31ZWhqvz) |

## risk

- **changeset included** — patch level, \`@vultisig/mpc-native\" only
- **ios unchanged** — swift side not touched, no new bindings
- **android is already non-functional** — can't bork what's already borked 🤷

## followups (not blocking)

the absolute-path \`DT_NEEDED\" issue that was in the old \`libgoschnorrswig.so\" is a dkls-android build infra bug. the production aars don't have it, but it would be nice to fix in the upstream build pipeline so we don't re-regress next bump.

items.title: Qemu in a unicorn like python wrapper
Full-stack security audit: fix 33 vulnerabilities across Python, Rust, and C/C++ layers
items.body:
Comprehensive security audit across the entire Zexus interpreter codebase spanning three rounds. Identified and fixed 33 vulnerabilities in Python source, Rust core, C/C++ native extensions, shell scripts, and Node.js scripts. Each fix includes a corresponding exploit test payload.

## Round 1 — Python Interpreter Core (11 vulnerabilities)

- **Command injection** in `stdlib/os_module.py` — `shell=True` with unsanitized input → forced `shell=False` with `shlex.split`
- **Pickle deserialization RCE** in `stdlib/fuzz_module.py` — replaced `pickle.loads` with JSON
- **`eval()` injection** in `vm/jit.py` — replaced `eval()` with `_safe_parse_number` using `ast.literal_eval`
- **Path traversal** in `vm/filesystem.py` (VFS prefix collision bypass), `evaluator/evaluator.py`, `stdlib/module_manager.py`
- **Environment variable leakage** in `stdlib/os_module.py` — added sensitive key filtering
- **Tarball symlink escape** in `zpm/installer.py`

## Round 2 — Rust Core, Scripts & Remaining Python (16 vulnerabilities)

- **`scripts/zpm.py`** — `shell=True` command injection, unquoted shim variables, PYTHONPATH hijacking
- **`scripts/zx-luncher.py`** — TOCTOU/symlink race condition
- **`security.py`** — unvalidated file export paths allowing writes outside CWD
- **`stdlib/template.py`** — path traversal in `render_file()`
- **`vm/binary_bytecode.py`** — pickle serialization fallback → replaced with `repr()`
- **`evaluator/unified_execution.py`** — predictable temp file path → `tempfile.mkstemp`
- **`external_bridge.py`** — hardcoded placeholder crypto keys → `secrets.token_hex`
- **`testing/zpics.py`, `zpics_runtime.py`** — path traversal via unsanitized `test_name`
- **Rust core** — gas metering overflow (`checked_add`), bytecode deserialization without size limits, `unwrap()` panics in contract_vm/merkle/state_adapter, negative index cast in rust_vm

## Round 3 — C/C++ Native Extension Layer (6 vulnerabilities)

- **Arbitrary file read/write** in `native_runtime.cpp` and `cabi.c` — no path validation on `read()`/`write()`, allowing `/etc/passwd` or `../../` traversal

```c
// Before: accepts any path
PyObject *args = PyTuple_Pack(2, path, PyUnicode_FromString("r"));

// After: validates path, fixes memory leak
if (!zx_validate_path(path)) { Py_RETURN_NONE; }
PyObject *mode = PyUnicode_FromString("r");
PyObject *args = PyTuple_Pack(2, path, mode);
Py_DECREF(mode);
```

- **Unrestricted module import** — `PyImport_Import()` with no restrictions lets JIT code import `os`/`subprocess`/`ctypes` → added 21-module blocklist with submodule matching
- **Use-after-free in `atomic_add`** — `Py_DECREF(delta_val)` before `PyNumber_Add(current, delta_val)` → deferred decref via `delta_owned` pattern
- **Memory leaks (10 locations)** — inline `PyUnicode_FromString()` passed to `PyTuple_Pack`/`PyDict_SetItemString` never decref'd
- **Negative gas charge bypass** — negative amount increases remaining gas → added sign validation
- **Duplicate declarations in `cabi.h`** — removed

## C/C++ Module Use Cases

| Module | Role |
|---|---|
| `native_runtime.cpp` | C++ JIT function pointer symbols for LLVM backend |
| `cabi.c` | Portable C ABI bridge (Python-callable + JIT pointers) |
| `cabi.h` | Stable ABI header shared by C/C++ implementations |
| `fastops.pyx` | Cython-accelerated bytecode dispatch loop |

## Verification

- 19 exploit test payloads, 46/46 checks pass
- 115 existing tests pass, 0 regressions (1 pre-existing failure from missing `pycryptodome`)

<!-- This is an auto-generated description by cubic. -->
---
## Summary by cubic
Fixes 33 security vulnerabilities across Python, Rust, and C/C++ layers to close RCE, path traversal, and sandbox bypass issues. Adds exploit tests and an audit report; all existing tests pass with no regressions.

- **Bug Fixes**
  - Python sandboxing: removed shell execution in `zpm run` and `os.execute`, filtered sensitive env vars, added AST-safe evaluation in debugger/accelerator/JIT, sanitized snapshot names, secured temp files, and blocked traversal in VFS, evaluator, module manager, templates, and ZPM installer.
  - JIT/bytecode: replaced `eval()`-based folding with strict numeric parsing and removed pickle-based constant serialization.
  - Rust core: prevented gas overflow with `checked_add`, added bytecode size limits, replaced `unwrap()` panics with errors, and hardened Merkle/state handling.
  - Native C/C++: validated paths for read/write, added import blocklist for dangerous modules, fixed a use-after-free and multiple leaks, and rejected negative gas charges.
  - Scripts: `postinstall.js` now sanitizes command names; `zx-luncher.py` resolves symlinks and only accepts `.zx` files.
  - Cryptography: `generate_sphincs_keypair` now returns runtime-random placeholders via `secrets`.

- **Migration**
  - `zpm run` and `os.execute` no longer run through a shell; scripts must be simple commands without shell operators.
  - File operations accept only in-project paths; absolute or traversal paths are rejected (templates, evaluator, VFS, audit export, installer).
  - JIT/native code cannot import dangerous Python modules (e.g., os, subprocess, ctypes); remove such imports.
  - Pickled constants are no longer supported in bytecode; rebuild artifacts that relied on pickle.

<sup>Written for commit 1aefd33e546f531fbc2c15608a7c919b0d13c890. Summary will update on new commits.</sup>

items.title: Add standalone Tkinter app for Nanotec stepper control
items.body:
### Motivation
- Provide a small, standalone, clickable Tkinter application that only controls the two Nanotec stepper motors so operators can run motor-only workflows without the full main GUI. 
- Keep integration-safe behavior by reusing the existing Nanotec protocol driver and keeping Taster/Shutter state manual until hardware I/O is fully wired. 

### Description
- Added a new standalone app `python_rewrite/nanotec_motor_app.py` that builds a two‑motor UI, manual Taster checkboxes, a manual Shutter input, encoder rotation indicators, process buttons for reference/infeed/outfeed/transfer and a sputter-run loop. 
- The app reuses the existing driver stack (`sputtering_app.devices.nanotec`, `SerialDeviceTransport` / `SerialSettings`) to start/stop/profile/poll motors and to remain compatible with NanoPro motor configuration. 
- Implemented encoder‑lamp logic that detects encoder motion from readback deltas and shows a green/red lamp, and implemented event logging to `python_rewrite/nanotec_motor_events.log` for Start/Stop/STEP/LIMIT/MOTOR_STOP events. 
- Added clickable launchers `Start_Nanotec_Motors.bat` and `Start_Nanotec_Motors.command` and updated `python_rewrite/README.md` with usage notes for the standalone app. 

### Testing
- Compiled the new script with `python3 -m py_compile python_rewrite/nanotec_motor_app.py` and the compilation succeeded. 
- No further automated unit tests were run against the new UI; the change is limited to a new standalone module and docs.

------

items.title: fix: use ctypes CGEventTap to eliminate PyObjC bridge event retention
items.body:
## Summary

- **Root cause**: PyObjC's `CGEventTapCreate` callback bridge internally retains `CGEventRef` wrappers — `leaks` confirms they're reachable (not leaked), held by the bridge's internal bookkeeping. ~42 MB (180K HIDEvent + 94K CGEvent) accumulates over 7 hours.
- **Fix**: New `wenzi/_cgeventtap.py` module provides pure ctypes bindings for `CGEventTapCreate`, `CFRunLoop`, and all `CGEvent*` functions. Callbacks receive raw `c_void_p` pointers — no PyObjC wrappers, no `CFRetain`, no retention.
- **Also**: Wrap `recognize_text()` in `objc.autorelease_pool()` to drain Vision framework objects on the OCR thread.

### Changed files
| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `_cgeventtap.py` | **New** — pure ctypes bindings (no PyObjC) |
| `hotkey.py` | 3 classes switched to ctypes CGEventTap |
| `snippet_expander.py` | Switched to ctypes CGEventTap |
| `ocr.py` | Added `autorelease_pool` |
| `test_hotkey.py` | Updated mocks for ctypes |
| `test_snippet_expander.py` | Updated for raw-pointer interface |

## Test plan

- [x] `uv run ruff check` — 0 errors
- [x] `uv run pytest tests/ -v` — 3759 passed
- [ ] Manual: run WenZi Lite for 1+ hours, `heap <pid> -s | grep HIDEvent` — count should stay near zero
- [ ] Manual: verify keyboard input, hotkeys, key remapping, snippet expansion all work

milestone.description: Fluent 2 design system alignment — lighter/brighter theme, typography fixes, spacing tokens, and component polish

items.body:
## Summary

Adds native Windows 11 Mica material backdrop to the main window. Mica samples the desktop wallpaper to create a subtle, personalized background that blends with the system theme — a key Fluent 2 design principle.

## New File: `app/mica.py`
- `enable_mica(hwnd, dark_mode)` — enables Mica via DwmSetWindowAttribute
- `enable_acrylic(hwnd, dark_mode)` — enables Acrylic (blur-behind)
- `disable_backdrop(hwnd)` — removes backdrop
- `is_mica_supported()` — checks Windows 11 Build 22621+

## Updated: `app/main_window.py`
Mica is applied after theme setup (calls enable_mica when on supported Windows build).

## Graceful degradation
- Windows 10 or non-Windows: no effect, standard solid background
- Invalid HWND: caught silently, logged at DEBUG level

## Reference
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/apps/design/style/mica

Closes #114

items.title: Remove testhelper, test SimpleArrayPlex directly
items.body:
## Summary

- Remove the C++ `TestSimpleArrayHelper` pybind binding and the entire `cpp/modmesh/testhelper/` directory
- Remove the `USE_PYTEST_HELPER_BINDING` CMake option and all references in CI workflows
- Remove `testhelper` submodule registration from `module.cpp` and `core.py`
- Replace the old `test_SimpleArray_casting` (which required the C++ helper) with new pytest-only test cases in `SimpleArrayPlexTC`

Close #720

## Old vs new test coverage

The old `test_SimpleArray_casting` method (in `SimpleArrayBasicTC`) relied on `TestSimpleArrayHelper` C++ pybind bindings. All scenarios it covered are now tested directly using the `.typed`, `.plex`, and `ndarray` Python APIs:

| Old test scenario (`test_SimpleArray_casting`) | New test case (`SimpleArrayPlexTC`) |
|---|---|
| All plex instances share `_modmesh.SimpleArray` type regardless of dtype (`type(plex_int32) is type(plex_float64)`) | `test_SimpleArrayPlex_uniform_type` |
| Plex with matching dtype accepted by C++ function (`helper.test_load_arrayin32_from_arrayplex(plex_int32)`) | `test_SimpleArrayPlex_cast_to_typed` — verifies `.typed` produces the correct typed class and `ndarray.dtype` matches |
| Plex with wrong dtype rejected by C++ function (`helper.test_load_arrayin32_from_arrayplex(plex_uint64)` → `TypeError`) | `test_SimpleArrayPlex_cast_dtype_mismatch` — verifies constructing a typed array from a wrong-dtype plex's ndarray raises `RuntimeError: dtype mismatch` |
| C++ cast returns correct typed class (`helper.test_cast_to_arrayint32()` matches `SimpleArrayInt32`) | `test_SimpleArrayPlex_typed_from_typed_constructor` — verifies typed from `plex.typed` matches typed from direct constructor |
| *(not previously covered)* | `test_SimpleArrayPlex_typed_roundtrip` — verifies plex → typed → plex type chain |
| *(not previously covered)* | `test_SimpleArrayPlex_typed_preserves_data` — verifies data integrity through `.typed` cast |

## Files removed

- `cpp/modmesh/testhelper/CMakeLists.txt`
- `cpp/modmesh/testhelper/pymod/testbuffer_pymod.hpp`

- `modmesh/core.py` — remove `list_of_testhelper` and references
- `tests/test_buffer.py` — remove old test, add 6 new direct test cases
- `.github/workflows/devbuild.yml` — remove `USE_PYTEST_HELPER_BINDING` flags, consolidate redundant ON/OFF build steps

items.title: Story 1.1: Python IEC 61850 GOOSE publisher/subscriber with ctypes libIEC61850 wrapper
items.body:
Bootstraps the repo with a pure-Python IEC 61850 GOOSE library (Story 1.1) that wraps libIEC61850 via ctypes when installed and falls back to a raw-socket implementation otherwise. Also delivers the project skeleton, CI pipeline, and glossary (Story 7.4).

## Core library — `iec61850/goose/`

- **`types.py`** — `GooseConfig`, `GooseDataEntry`, `GooseDataType` (IEC 61850-8-1 Table A.2 BER tags), `GooseFrame` dataclasses
- **`codec.py`** — Pure-Python BER encoder/decoder for full GOOSE PDUs; no native dependency required
- **`publisher.py`** — `GoosePublisher`: auto-selects ctypes path (libIEC61850) or raw `AF_PACKET` socket; manages `stNum`/`sqNum` lifecycle
- **`subscriber.py`** — `GooseSubscriber`: daemon thread with APPID filter and typed callback
- **`_libiec61850.py`** — ctypes bindings; auto-discovers library via `$LIBIEC61850_PATH` → `find_library` → common paths; degrades gracefully when absent

```python
from iec61850.goose import GoosePublisher, GooseConfig, GooseDataEntry

pub = GoosePublisher(GooseConfig(interface="eth0", appid=0x0001))
pub.publish([GooseDataEntry.boolean(True), GooseDataEntry.float32(1500.0)])
pub.retransmit([GooseDataEntry.boolean(True), GooseDataEntry.float32(1500.0)])
pub.close()
```

## Tests & example

- **`tests/test_goose.py`** — 51 unit tests (BER round-trips, publisher/subscriber with mocked sockets, full frame integration); no hardware or root required
- **`examples/trip_on_overcurrent.py`** — simulated ANSI 50/51 overcurrent relay; publishes GOOSE trip + retransmissions; `--dry-run` mode needs no privileges

## Infrastructure

- **`.github/workflows/ci.yml`** — lint (flake8) + pytest matrix (Python 3.9–3.12) + Docker build; `contents: read` permissions
- **`pyproject.toml`** — installable package (`pip install -e ".[dev]"`)
- **`Dockerfile`**, **`README.md`** (quickstart, architecture diagram), **`CONTRIBUTING.md`**, **`LICENSE`**

## Docs

- **`docs/glossary.md`** — 25+ terms: IED, GOOSE, MMS, SV, SCL, PTOC/PTRC/XCBR, ADMS, DNP3, COMTRADE, PTP, merging unit, logical node, data object, etc.

# 📋 User Stories — Open Source Grid Operations & Substation Automation

> **Repo Purpose:** A community-driven open-source project providing reusable tools, libraries, and reference implementations for grid operations technology — covering substation automation, SCADA integration, IEC 61850, data pipelines, and grid modernization.

-----

## 🏷️ Labels Used

- `epic` — parent theme
- `story` — individual user story
- `good first issue` — suitable for new contributors
- `priority: high / medium / low`
- `domain: IEC61850 / SCADA / data / OT-security / analytics / comms`

-----

## EPIC 1 — IEC 61850 Communication Foundation

> Enable open, standards-compliant IEC 61850 communication between IEDs, merging units, and control systems.

-----

**Story 1.1** `story` `domain: IEC61850` `priority: high`
**As a** protection engineer,
**I want** a Python wrapper around libIEC61850 that publishes and subscribes to GOOSE messages,
**So that** I can prototype protection logic without writing C code.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

- [ ] Wrap libIEC61850 GOOSE publisher/subscriber using ctypes or cffi
- [ ] Support configurable dataset, APPID, and multicast MAC
- [ ] Include unit tests with simulated GOOSE frames
- [ ] Provide a working example: publish a trip signal on overcurrent condition

-----

**Story 1.2** `story` `domain: IEC61850` `priority: high`
**As a** substation automation engineer,
**I want** a utility to parse and validate SCL files (ICD, CID, SCD),
**So that** I can programmatically inspect IED data models and logical node configurations.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

- [ ] Parse SCL XML and expose Logical Devices, Logical Nodes, Data Objects
- [ ] Validate against IEC 61850-6 schema
- [ ] Output structured JSON or Python dataclass representation
- [ ] CLI tool: `scl-parse mysubstation.scd --output json`

-----

**Story 1.3** `story` `domain: IEC61850` `priority: medium`
**As a** test engineer,
**I want** a simulated IED environment using Docker,
**So that** I can run end-to-end IEC 61850 tests without physical hardware.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**So that** I can diagnose communication issues during commissioning.

> Extract, normalize, and load substation telemetry and event data into modern data platforms for analytics and AI/ML.

**Story 2.1** `story` `domain: data` `priority: high`
**As a** data engineer,

**So that** substation telemetry is available for analytics without manual extraction.

- [ ] Normalize report data to a flat schema: `[timestamp, ied_id, logical_node, data_object, value, quality]`

- [ ] Configurable via YAML (IED IP, report control block, Snowflake target)

**Story 2.2** `story` `domain: data` `priority: high`

- [ ] Extract: analog channels (V, I), digital channels (relay outputs), timestamps, sampling rate

- [ ] CLI: `comtrade-parse fault_20240312.cfg --output parquet --dest s3://bucket/faults/`

items.title: fix: block FFI/ctypes sandbox bypass via attribute chain access
items.body:
## Summary
- **P0 Security Fix**: Block sandbox escape via `numpy.ctypeslib.ctypes.CDLL(None).system()` and similar FFI attribute chains
- Add `validate_no_ffi_access()` AST validator that blocks `ctypeslib`, `ctypes`, `CDLL`/`cdll`/`windll`/`oledll`/`PyDLL`, `cffi`/`FFI`, and `dlopen` — both dot notation and string-based bracket notation
- Integrate FFI validation into `_validate_user_code()` pipeline (runs after import and dunder checks)
- Add 20 new tests covering all blocked FFI paths, string-based access, full attack chains, and safe code passthrough

## Root Cause
The existing import whitelist blocked `import ctypes` but not attribute-chain access via allowed modules. Since `numpy` is whitelisted, `numpy.ctypeslib.ctypes.CDLL(None).system('id')` could load libc and execute arbitrary OS commands.

## Test plan
- [x] 16 unit tests for `validate_no_ffi_access()` (each blocked attr, string-based, safe passthrough)
- [x] 4 integration tests in `TestExecuteCode` (ctypeslib chain, ctypes import, cffi import, CDLL chain)
- [x] `make check` passes (124 backend tests, 58 frontend tests, 95% coverage)

items.title: cgt: wire Python wrapper into Bazel build
items.body:
## Problem

`cgt-py.i` and the CMake `swig_lib(NAME cgt_py ...)` target existed, but the Bazel build was missing the corresponding `python_wrap_cc` target and the `%include` in `OpenRoad-py.i`. As a result, when using the `ord_py` pip package, `design.getClockGating()` returned an untyped SWIG proxy and calling any method on it (e.g. `.run()`) failed — even though the CGT C++ code was already linked into `_openroadpy.so`.

Every other tool (ant, cts, fin, grt, ...) had its `swig-py` target wired in; cgt was the one missing.

## Fix

Three purely additive changes, following the identical pattern used by `fin`, `tap`, and all other tools:

1. **`src/cgt/BUILD`** — add `python_wrap_cc(name = "swig-py", ...)` target
2. **`BUILD.bazel`** — add `//src/cgt:swig-py` to `openroad_swig-py` deps
3. **`src/OpenRoad-py.i`** — add `%include "src/cgt/src/cgt-py.i"` inside the `#ifdef BAZEL` block

After this fix `design.getClockGating()` returns a typed `cgt::ClockGating` Python object and all its methods (`run()`, `setMinInstances()`, `setMaxCover()`, `setDumpDir()`) work correctly.

## Testing

`//src/cgt:swig-py` builds successfully with Bazelisk:

```
bazelisk build //src/cgt:swig-py  →  Build completed successfully
```

All 6 CGT tests (3 Tcl + 3 Python) pass via CMake:

```
cgt.aes_nangate45.tcl   PASSED
cgt.aes_nangate45.py    PASSED
cgt.countdown_asap7.tcl PASSED
cgt.countdown_asap7.py  PASSED
cgt.ibex_sky130hd.tcl   PASSED
cgt.ibex_sky130hd.py    PASSED
```

items.title: Add `__callback__` property to weakref type
items.body:
RustPython's `weakref` type was missing the `__callback__` read-only property, blocking PyO3 weakref compatibility (e.g. `reference.getattr("__callback__")` fails).

### Changes

- **`crates/vm/src/object/core.rs`**: Add `get_callback()` to `PyWeak` — reads the callback field under the stripe lock using the same double-check pattern as `upgrade()`
- **`crates/vm/src/builtins/weakref.rs`**: Expose `__callback__` as a read-only `#[pygetset]`
- **`Lib/test/test_weakref.py`**: Remove `expectedFailure` from `test_callback_attribute` and `test_callback_attribute_after_deletion`
- **`extra_tests/snippets/stdlib_weakref.py`**: Add regression coverage

```python
>>> import weakref
>>> class C: pass
>>> o = C()
>>> weakref.ref(o).__callback__ is None
True
>>> cb = lambda r: None
>>> weakref.ref(o, cb).__callback__ is cb
True
```

Semantics match CPython: returns the callback if alive, `None` if no callback was set or referent was collected (callback consumed during clear).

items.title: feat: framework-agnostic GPU detection via ctypes (replaces torch.cuda + nvidia-smi)
items.body:
## Summary

- **GPU detection via CUDA Driver API** — `detect_gpu()` uses `cuInit`, `cuDeviceGetCount`, `cuDeviceGetName` via ctypes. No torch import required in the core framework.
- **GPU metrics via NVML** — `get_gpu_metrics()` uses `nvmlDeviceGetUtilizationRates`, `nvmlDeviceGetMemoryInfo` via ctypes. Replaces `nvidia-smi` subprocess calls (faster, no PATH dependency).
- **GPU library preloading** — Preloads `libcuda.so.1` and `libnvidia-ml.so.1` with `RTLD_GLOBAL` via absolute paths before any framework import. Fixes CUDA init failures on GKE where `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is not set at process startup.
- **Zero torch imports in core** — `openfilter/filter_runtime/` no longer imports torch. Individual filters still import torch in their own `setup()`.
- **Secondary torch validation** — When torch is installed, `_validate_device()` also checks `torch.cuda.is_available()` to catch CPU-only wheels or incompatible CUDA runtimes.
- **OPENFILTER_FORK=1 fix** — Now explicitly forces fork mode (previously only skipped spawn, relying on platform default).

## New file: `openfilter/filter_runtime/gpu.py`

| Function | Replaces | API |
|---|---|---|
| `preload_gpu_libs()` | `_preload_gpu_libs()` in filter.py | ctypes.CDLL with RTLD_GLOBAL |
| `detect_gpu()` → `GPUInfo` | `torch.cuda.is_available()`, `torch.cuda.device_count()` | CUDA Driver API |
| `get_gpu_metrics()` → `list[GPUMetrics]` | `subprocess.run(['nvidia-smi', ...])` | NVML |
| `nvml_shutdown()` | N/A | Clean NVML state on exit |

## Why

The openfilter framework should not depend on torch for GPU detection. Filters using TensorFlow, ONNX Runtime, or custom CUDA code shouldn't need torch installed just to detect GPUs.

## Review fixes (v0.1.28)

- `_find_gpu_lib` no longer returns a bare name fallback - returns `None`, callers use `path or "libcuda.so.1"` to try the bare name
- `set_start_method` moved from import-time side effect to `_configure_start_method()` called at top of `cmd_run()`
- `NvmlUtilization`/`NvmlMemory` ctypes structures moved to module level (avoid recreation per call)
- `preload_gpu_libs()` now populates `_libcuda`/`_libnvml` cache to avoid double-loading

## Tests

34 tests covering GPU detection, NVML metrics, preloading, and device validation (including driver-present-but-torch-unavailable scenarios).

## Test plan

- [x] 34 GPU tests pass
- [x] 379 other tests pass (9 pre-existing failures unrelated)
- [x] ruff check + format clean
- [ ] Deploy to GKE and verify torch.cuda.is_available() == True
- [ ] Verify GPU metrics in Grafana dashboard (NVML vs old nvidia-smi values)

items.title: Add AirSpy ctypes wrapper and migrate find_capsule to pyrtlsdr (RTL-SDR) backend
items.body:
### Motivation

- Provide a lightweight Windows `ctypes` wrapper for `airspy.dll` to open devices, configure basic parameters, and collect IQ samples into NumPy arrays.  
- Migrate the `find_capsule.py` signal-finder tool from the AirSpy-specific API to a more common RTL-SDR backend using `pyrtlsdr` to support cheaper dongles and simplify runtime dependencies.

### Description

- Add `airspy_wrapper.py` implementing a minimal `AirSpy` class that wraps `airspy.dll` signatures, exposes `open`, `close`, `start`, `stop`, and configuration methods, converts interleaved `int16` IQ to `complex64`, and raises `AirSpyError` on nonzero return codes.  
- Replace AirSpy usage in `find_capsule.py` with a new `RtlReader` class that uses `pyrtlsdr.RtlSdr` and provides `configure`, `capture`, and `close` methods.  
- Refactor `measure_power` to accept a `RtlReader` and return dBm from captured samples, add `gain_to_rtl_gain` to map the previous user gain range to RTL-SDR dB gain, and set a stable `SAMPLE_RATE`.  
- Add runtime import guard for `pyrtlsdr`, improve error messages, adjust frequency validation range, remove/ignore bias-T control for the pyrtlsdr backend, update CLI help text and status output, and ensure the RTL reader is closed in a `finally` block.

### Testing

|---|---|
| `.gitignore` | Union: `.claude/` (PR) + `*.json`/`llm_checkpoints`/`logs`/etc (master) |
| `src/jstar/codegen.rs` | Kept PR's `StrCopy` in the void-dest match arm |

Also carries in `src/jstar/optimizer.rs` (new file, PR-only) and `src/jstar/typechecker.rs` (clean auto-merge from PR).

> **Note:** Branch protection rules on `claude/continue-session-TSmxK` prevented a direct push to the PR head. Resolved content is on `copilot/fix-codeql-cpp-analysis` — the repo admin can merge this branch to master or reset the PR head from it.

Extraction failed: No source files found." Assistant: The failing job **70933084862** is the **CodeQL** workflow running **C/C++** analysis (`/language:c-cpp`). It fails during the CodeQL **autobuild** step because the repository contains **no C/C++ source files**, so extraction can’t start:

- `Indexed folder ".../apps", found 0 source files, 0 header files...`

### Fix options (pick one)

#### Option A (recommended): Stop running C/C++ CodeQL for this repo
Update the CodeQL workflow config to remove `cpp` (and `c-cpp`) from the language matrix / init step so CodeQL only analyzes the languages that actually exist in this repo (e.g., `javascript-typescript`, `python`, etc.).

**Code change (example):**
```yaml

strategy:
  matrix:
    language: [ 'javascript-typescript' ]  # remove 'cpp' / 'c-cpp'
```

or if it’s using the `codeql-action/init` languages list:

```yaml
- uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3

    languages: javascript-typescript  # do not include cpp
```

This resolves the failure by preventing CodeQL from attempting C/C++ extraction.

#### Option B: Keep C/C++ scanning, but point it at real C/C++ code
Only do this if the repo is *supposed* to contain C/C++ but CodeQL isn’t seeing it (e.g., code lives in a submodule, generated during build, or in a different directory). In that case:
- Ensure C/C++ files are actually present in the workspace at analysis time.
- Provide an explicit build step (CodeQL “manual build”) that compiles the project so extraction can observe the build.

**Code change (example):**
```yaml
- uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3

    languages: cpp

- name: Build (manual)

    cmake -S . -B build

```

The job is not failing due to a compilation error—it's failing because CodeQL C/C++ autobuild cannot proceed when **no `.c/.cc/.cpp/.h/...` files are found**. Removing the C/C++ language from CodeQL (Option A) is the clean solution unless C/C++ code is genuinely expected to be analyzed.

items.title: Use Python c api instead of ctypes
feat: replace hip-python and AMDSymmetricMemory with new SymmetricMemory
items.body:
## Why custom `SymmetricMemory`  ?

### API path comparison

The torch-native [`CUDASymmetricMemory`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/358117c166b75167a09bca81ac9925940feda339/torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/symm_mem/CUDASymmetricMemory.cu#L435) on ROCm uses the **HIP VMM** path:

```
hipMemCreate(hipMemAllocationTypePinned)
  → hipMemExportToShareableHandle (POSIX FD)
  → hipMemImportFromShareableHandle
  → hipMemAddressReserve → hipMemMap → hipMemSetAccess
```

Our custom implementation uses the **HIP IPC** path:

```
hipMalloc
  → hipIpcGetMemHandle → all_gather_object → hipIpcOpenMemHandle
```

`hipIpcOpenMemHandle` directly creates a hardware-level XGMI peer mapping via the KFD driver in a single call. The VMM path goes through the DMA-BUF subsystem and a more complex virtual address management layer, which on current ROCm may not produce equally optimized XGMI peer mappings.

### Potential bug in torch ROCm path

The [`map_block`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/358117c166b75167a09bca81ac9925940feda339/torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/symm_mem/CUDASymmetricMemoryUtils.cpp) ROCm implementation calls `hipMemMap` **twice** on the same address — a copy-paste bug (the CUDA path has a single `cuMemMap` call):

```cpp
C10_CUDA_CHECK(hipMemAddressReserve(ptr, size, 0ULL, 0, 0ULL));
C10_CUDA_CHECK(hipMemMap(*ptr, size, 0, handle, 0ULL));
C10_CUDA_CHECK(hipMemMap(*ptr, size, 0, handle, 0ULL));  // ← duplicate
```

This may cause the driver to re-establish the mapping with degraded page-table attributes.

### Benchmark

Intranode allgather on 8× MI300X, running the **same Triton kernel** on both backends:

```
python benchmark/ops/bench_symmetric_memory.py
```

| nbytes | Backend | Latency (max rank) | BW (min rank) | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 MB | `symm_mem.py` | 0.044 ms | 191.5 GB/s | **7.4×** |
| 1 MB | `torch._symmetric_memory` | 0.325 ms | 25.8 GB/s | — |
| 2 MB | `symm_mem.py` | 0.055 ms | 306.6 GB/s | **12.4×** |
| 2 MB | `torch._symmetric_memory` | 0.679 ms | 24.7 GB/s | — |
| 4 MB | `symm_mem.py` | 0.113 ms | 297.2 GB/s | **11.8×** |
| 4 MB | `torch._symmetric_memory` | 1.338 ms | 25.1 GB/s | — |

The custom implementation is **7–12× faster** on the same workload, with all correctness checks passing.
